result.position: YES to a hard spec-size target — but scoped to a small mandatory kernel, not the whole spec. The language should ship a layered specification: a tiny always-in-context kernel (canonical surface, concrete types, checked ownership in a restricted kernel discipline, calls, monomorphized generic instantiation, sum-type match, loops, Result+trap, a minimal named-mode subset) under a hard, real-tokenizer-measured token budget, plus on-demand layer specs (full numeric-mode ladder, FFI wall, storage/region contracts, contract groups, provenance/generator tooling) retrieved per task. The language must be architected so that most generated code units need only the kernel, and that property must be a measured, policed design target, not an aspiration.
result.coreArgument:
1) D2 creates two budgets; a monolithic hard cap conflates them and rations safety against coverage. D2 makes spec-token cost a first-class constraint, but the spec is not homogeneous: some rules are load-bearing for every program (ownership/aliasing — the D1 machinery that F001/C004 show is the core optimizer-and-safety enabler; dispatch; canonical form), while others are load-bearing only for programs that touch them (the N001/N002/N004/N005 numeric-fact ladder, FFI attenuation, region/arena contracts). A single hard cap over the whole spec forces exactly the wrong trade: to fit under the ceiling you compress the UB-adjacent periphery — the numeric-modes and fact-channel text whose under-specification is how silent miscompiles enter (N001–N005), or you compress the D1 ownership text, which is non-negotiable. A kernel-scoped hard cap keeps the forcing function where it does good (the always-paid cost every single task carries) without ever putting 'shorten the noalias rules' and 'shorten the FFI attenuation rules' in budget competition.

2) The round-2 minimal core already stratifies this way; layering is reading the synthesis honestly, not redesigning it. The minimal-core sketch cleanly separates an always-needed nucleus (SURFACE canonical form, TYPES/ownership, GENERICS mono-only with explicit instantiation, DISPATCH via exhaustive match, ERRORS Result+trap=abort) from vocabularies that are named-and-closed but task-specific: the numeric-mode vocabulary ('a small vocabulary of named, truthfully-lowered modes'), the single FFI wall (explicitly UNDESIGNED), the region/arena layer (to-be-carded), the contract-group kernel (undesigned). The undesigned/pending items are all in layers — proof they evolve on a different cadence than the kernel. A whole-spec hard cap would make every one of those pending designs a zero-sum fight against frozen kernel text; a kernel-only cap lets them land as additive layers. Critically, no round-2 floor needs reopening: canonical form, closed-world, Result+trap, no implicit dispatch, checked ownership, mono-only generics, and zero implicit conversions all live in the kernel, and layering is a TEACHING artifact — a partition of spec text — not a semantic module system, so the modules cut (D004/D007/D008 rationale) is untouched: the compiler still sees one closed whole program.

3) The floors actively make layering enforceable rather than hopeful. Because the stored artifact is byte-canonical and fully elaborated (round2-syntax 'what must be true'), every construct whose semantics live in a layer is lexically self-identifying: mode names on arithmetic nodes, FFI extern regions, storage-contract annotations. Layer membership is therefore a syntactic, content-addressable fact. That yields the architecture this position forces: (a) the compiler derives a per-code-unit LAYER MANIFEST (which layers this unit's constructs draw on) purely from canonical bytes; (b) the toolchain enforces a teaching contract — the writer's context must have contained the manifest's layer specs, analogous to the synthesis's contract-edit gating; (c) diagnostics gain a spec-anchor field (machine-readable node paths already being an obligation) pointing at the exact kernel or layer rule violated, so the repair loop retrieves precisely the text needed — this operationalizes D2's 'compiler-as-teacher progressive disclosure' option with a checkable mechanism instead of vibes. And because non-canonical input is a hard error and the grammar is closed, a writer that hallucinates an untaught layer construct gets a deterministic rejection, not D009-style silent degradation — the failure mode of teaching-by-parts is loud by construction.

4) What else it forces architecturally — these are commitments, not free wins: (i) CONSERVATIVE-EXTENSION LAW: layers may only add named vocabulary (new modes, new contract forms, new annotation spellings); they may never alter the meaning of any kernel construct, so a kernel-only reading of a program that uses no layer constructs is its true and final semantics. Without this, code written under kernel-only context changes meaning when a layer loads — a teaching-level analog of the unsound-frontend-fact problem (F003 negative scope). (ii) KERNEL-MODE SUBSET: the zero-implicit-conversion floor says every arithmetic node names exactly one mode, so the kernel must carry a minimal default mode set (one checked integer mode, one FP mode) with the full ladder in the numerics layer — otherwise no arithmetic is writable kernel-only and the 'most code needs only the kernel' target is dead on arrival. (iii) RESTRICTED KERNEL OWNERSHIP DISCIPLINE: the kernel teaches own/&/&mut with second-class, non-escaping borrows and no interior mutability; richer region/arena/lifetime vocabulary is the memory layer. Kernel-only programs are strictly more restricted, hence still meet the D1 bar; this is the F006 SPARK pattern — safety via restriction — applied to spec size, and it is the only credible answer to 'ownership text is too big for any kernel budget'. (iv) KERNEL-CHANGE GATING: because kernel tokens are paid by every task forever, kernel edits are privileged operations (like contract edits); new features land as layers by default. (v) SPEC-AS-ARTIFACT: the spec itself becomes canonical, content-addressed, versioned with the compiler, with layer specs generated from a single source of record — otherwise kernel/layer drift reproduces in the spec the near-duplicate drift the consistency layer polices in code. (vi) ANTI-GAMING METRIC: the hard kernel cap is paired with a pre-committed census threshold — the fraction of real generated code units whose layer manifest is empty must stay above a floor — so semantics cannot be quietly relocated into layers to nominally meet the cap while all real work requires them.

5) Why not the alternatives. No target at all: D2 says the spec budget is first-class; with no hard number there is no forcing function, and spec growth is unpoliced in exactly the way the synthesis warns the periphery is already accreting ('a second, evidence-free language'). Full-spec-always-in-context: pays the entire spec on every task, and its defense rests on the same untested model-behavior claims as mine (that long teaching contexts don't degrade adherence — the metaprogramming verdict records 'no cards exist' for context-length effects) while forfeiting the architectural discipline a kernel cap buys; note also that even if prompt caching amortizes raw token cost, cost is not the only channel — attention/adherence dilution is the live (untested) concern, and the kernel cap hedges both. Whole-spec hard cap: rations safety text against periphery text (point 1) and structurally punishes closing the corpus's named holes (FFI, regions, contract groups), each of which must eventually ADD spec text. The layered-kernel position is the only one that keeps a hard number where it disciplines design, keeps D1 text uncompressed, keeps the pending holes closable, reopens no floor, and converts progressive disclosure from an unfalsifiable hope into a manifest-checked, census-measured mechanism with named experiments and pre-committed thresholds — the corpus's own standard (caveats.md standing rule) for how decisions are allowed to harden.

6) The number itself is set by experiment, not taste: adopt the cap as LAW now (kernel spec + mandatory worked examples, measured with real model tokenizers per the round2-syntax follow-up methodology — not regex proxies), but fix its numeric value from the spec-ablation degradation curve (see EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED list), with a pre-committed procedure for the one legitimate reopening: if the restricted kernel ownership discipline cannot both meet D1 and fit any budget at which models perform acceptably, the kernel cap — not D1 — yields, and the position falls back to full-spec-in-context with the cap demoted to a spec-simplicity telemetry metric.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Ownership/anti-aliasing non-interference is the central optimizer enabler across Rust/Fortran/SPARK/G++ — this is the text that must be in every writer's context for every task, defining the kernel's non-negotiable core and why its spec weight cannot be rationed against periphery text under a whole-spec cap.

SPARK's true-by-construction-via-restriction pattern is the carded precedent for the kernel's restricted ownership discipline: kernel-only programs use a more restricted (second-class borrows, no interior mutability) discipline that still meets the D1 bar, keeping kernel spec text small; richer region vocabulary moves to the memory layer.

Send/Sync-class capability predicates are the type-level sharing vocabulary the kernel must name for D1 thread-safety; noting its corrected status (provisional, CONFIRMED_WITH_AMENDED_SOURCES 2026-07-02, not workflow-confirmed 3-0) — kernel content citing it inherits that caveat.

The UB/poison/undef ladder shows that under-specified numeric semantics are a silent-miscompile channel; a whole-spec hard cap that pressures compression of numeric-mode text risks exactly this, which is the core argument for capping the kernel only and giving numerics a full uncompressed layer.

Separation of default arithmetic results from optimization promises (nsw/nuw) is why every arithmetic node names a mode (decided floor) — forcing the kernel to include a minimal default-mode subset so kernel-only arithmetic is writable, with the full ladder as the numerics layer.
Range metadata as a UB-bearing fact channel exemplifies layer content that must be specified fully or not used at all — supporting layered full-fidelity specs over budget-compressed whole-spec text.
Inbounds GEP contracts are another UB-bearing channel in the same class as N004: periphery text where compression is dangerous, so it must live outside the hard-capped kernel.

Mono-only generics with compiler-side expansion (round-2 floor) keeps the writer-facing generic surface small — templates plus explicit instantiation is kernel-sized precisely because instantiation machinery is in the compiler, not the spec the model must read; flagged as priority-verify in the synthesis, so kernel content leaning on it inherits that risk.

Whole-program compilation and content-addressed caching mean spec layering is purely a teaching-artifact partition; the closed-world floor is untouched because the compiler still sees one program — layers never become semantic module boundaries.

Julia's silent-degradation-under-type-instability is the carded image of what teaching-by-parts must never do: an untaught construct must hard-error (canonical closed grammar), never degrade quietly — the loud-failure property that makes kernel-only generation safe.

Halide's schedule layer is carded precedent that a separable, optional vocabulary can sit atop a fixed core without changing core meaning — the structural template for conservative-extension layers (its negative scope against transplanting staged-DSL machinery is respected: layers add vocabulary, not stages).

ISPC's uniform/varying and target-parameter vocabulary shows hardware-specific teaching content is naturally task-conditional — retrieved for SIMD tasks, absent otherwise — a concrete example of layer content most code units never need.

Its negative scope (facts sound only if frontend obligations hold and survive lowering) is the analog justifying the conservative-extension law: layer text must never retroactively change kernel-construct meaning, or kernel-context-written code becomes unsound the way unverified frontend facts do.

HotSpot's conditional-not-guaranteed escape analysis is the corpus's standing warning against relying on unguaranteed recovery mechanisms — applied here: layer retrieval must be a checked contract (manifest enforcement), not an assumed-to-work convenience.

concessions: The corpus contains ZERO cards on spec size, in-context teachability, or AI codegen of novel languages; every card cited above supports this position structurally or by analogy, never directly. Under the caveats.md standing rule this position cannot harden into spec until the named experiments run.

The whole-spec-cap camp has one real virtue I must absorb: a kernel-only cap is gameable by relocating semantics into layers. I concede the cap is meaningless without the paired anti-gaming census metric (fraction of code units with empty layer manifests) adopted as law at the same time — the cap alone is not a sufficient ruling.

Ownership spec text is the genuine weak point: Rust-class borrow rules are the largest single teaching item, and if the restricted kernel discipline (second-class borrows, no interior mutability) either fails to meet D1 or fails to cover enough real code, the kernel budget is unachievable and the position degrades to full-spec-in-context. I pre-commit that fallback rather than defend the cap past that evidence.

Cross-layer interaction text (storage contracts x ownership, FFI x everything) can grow super-linearly with layer count; the conservative-extension law is easy to state and hard to prove for interacting vocabularies. The FFI layer in particular — the synthesis's own open tension notes the periphery is accreting into a second evidence-free language — may resist clean layering entirely.

If prompt caching amortizes one-time spec cost to near-zero in practice, the economic half of my argument weakens substantially and the position rests mainly on the untested attention/adherence-dilution claim plus the architectural-discipline benefits; that is a thinner case than the full steelman presents.
A soft whole-spec telemetry ceiling remains worth tracking even under my position — I concede total spec growth is a real cost channel that a kernel-only cap does not by itself police.
risks: Retrieval infrastructure becomes load-bearing and unverified: layer-manifest prediction, retrieval, and context-assembly join provenance-checked generators as toolchain components the soundness story silently depends on — the synthesis already flags provenance-record ergonomics as EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED; this adds a second such component.

Layer proliferation recreates the periphery problem: 'make it a layer' becomes the pressure valve for every contested feature, and the layer set accretes into exactly the second, evidence-free language the synthesis's open-tensions section warns about — kernel-change gating pushes design pressure outward by construction.

Spec drift between kernel and layers relocates the near-duplicate-drift problem from code (where the consistency layer polices it) into the spec itself; single-source spec generation with content addressing is mandatory tooling, and it does not exist.

First-generation failure cost: a writer that needs a layer it wasn't given produces hard-errored code and burns repair iterations; if layer-need prediction is unreliable, the multi-round repair loop could cost more total tokens than full-spec-in-context would have — the position could lose on its own metric.

The conservative-extension law may be unenforceable in practice for the memory layer: region/arena vocabulary that genuinely refines kernel ownership semantics (rather than adding beside them) would violate monotonicity, forcing either an uglier kernel or a law exception that unravels the guarantee.

Freezing a hard kernel number before the round-3 safety-envelope debate (no round-3 verdict files exist in the corpus yet) risks the cap being immediately broken by whatever proof-burden/escape-hatch obligations that debate places on writers — kernel-cap ratification should sequence after it.
empiricalUntested: CLAIM: Models generate a novel language more accurately from a small always-present kernel plus task-relevant retrieved layers than from a monolithic full spec at equal total capability. UNTESTED — no corpus card touches it. EXPERIMENT (spec-ablation A/B, the flagship for this ruling): identical task suites run with (a) kernel-only context, (b) kernel+correct layers, (c) full spec, (d) full spec compressed to kernel token count; measure first-parse success, silent semantic-error rate via differential testing, repair iterations to green, and total tokens including repair rounds; the degradation curve across kernel sizes sets the numeric cap.

CLAIM: Longer teaching contexts measurably raise AI defect rates or dilute rule adherence ('attention dilution'). UNTESTED — the round-2 metaprogramming verdict explicitly records 'no cards exist' for context-length effects. EXPERIMENT: fixed task, kernel rules held constant, pad context with valid-but-irrelevant layer text at increasing lengths; measure kernel-rule violation rate as a function of context size.

CLAIM: Models (or cheap static tooling over the task description) can reliably predict which layers a task needs before writing code. UNTESTED. EXPERIMENT: layer-manifest prediction — compare predicted manifests against compiler-derived post-hoc manifests over a workload corpus; measure miss rate and the repair-iteration cost per miss; a high miss rate is a pre-committed trigger for the full-spec fallback.

CLAIM: 'Most code needs only the kernel' — the load-bearing workload assumption of the entire position. UNTESTED. EXPERIMENT: workload census over representative optimizer-domain generated code, measuring the empty-manifest fraction and per-layer demand frequency; doubles as the anti-gaming metric; pre-commit the floor below which the kernel/layer boundary must be redrawn.

CLAIM: Spec-token count is the right budgeted quantity at all — in-context learning of a novel language may be dominated by worked examples rather than rule prose, making example count/quality the true budget currency. UNTESTED. EXPERIMENT: at fixed total token budget, vary the rules-to-examples ratio in the kernel spec; measure generation accuracy to find what the cap should actually meter.

CLAIM: Kernel-rule adherence is stable when layers are loaded (no cross-layer interference where layer examples cause drift in kernel-construct usage). UNTESTED. EXPERIMENT: kernel-only tasks executed with and without irrelevant layers present; measure kernel-construct error-rate delta.

CLAIM: Spec-anchored diagnostics (compiler-as-teacher pointing to the violated rule's text for retrieval) shorten repair loops versus diagnostics alone. UNTESTED — extends the synthesis's diagnostics repair-loop-asymmetry experiment. EXPERIMENT: repair tasks with node-path diagnostics alone vs diagnostics plus retrieved spec-anchor text; measure fix success rate and iterations.

CLAIM: The training-distribution penalty for the alien canonical form decays with in-context kernel examples fast enough to be paid within the kernel budget (couples to the round2-syntax A/B follow-up on familiarity leverage, which is D2's other open question). UNTESTED. EXPERIMENT: the already-ordered syntax A/B measuring penalty decay with example count, extended to report the token cost of examples needed to reach the plateau — that cost is a mandatory line item inside the kernel cap.

result.position: Regularity-first, familiarity where free: keep the round-2 canonical single-spelling machine grammar as law; admit familiar atoms (keywords, identifiers, literals, type names) only where every prior-driven misuse they invite is checker-loud; force visibly alien spellings wherever semantics diverge from the training prior, because a familiar surface over divergent semantics converts unfamiliarity errors (loud, repairable) into prior-driven errors (silent, shipped).
result.coreArgument:
1. THE LOUDNESS CRITERION — the decision procedure this position actually proposes. The familiarity question is not binary; it decomposes per surface element. Borrow a familiar spelling if and only if every misuse the training prior invites at that spelling is converted by the checker into a hard error; require an alien spelling wherever prior-driven misuse would be silent. This operationalizes 'familiarity where free.' Under the round-2 core, most Rust-prior misuse is already loud by construction: closures, wildcard arms, instantiation inference, and implicit conversions are inexpressible or hard errors (round2-synthesis-minimal-core.md cut list), so borrowing `fn`, `let`, `struct`, `enum`, `match`, snake_case, and literal/type-name conventions costs repair iterations at worst — a bounded, measurable D2 loop cost. The silent zone is where the language keeps a construct Rust also has but with different semantics: arithmetic (N002 — overflow behavior is a per-operation optimizer CONTRACT; Rust's bare `+` prior is panic-in-debug/wrap-in-release, ours is a named mode per node), conversions (`as`-shaped casts carry Rust truncation/saturation priors against our zero-implicit-conversion floor, N001/D010), drop timing ('cleanup always runs' is explicitly NOT an invariant here, contradicting the deepest Rust prior), auto-derived Send/Sync (C004: capabilities are checked predicates here, and manual assertion is the documented UB channel), and Result-vs-trap classification. In exactly those places the spelling must be alien and mode-carrying (`add.wrap x y`, named conversion ops), so the prior cannot silently apply. A Rust-adjacent surface maximizes the one failure class every round-2 verdict is architected against: wrong-but-well-typed (round2-type-inference.md's silent semantic decisions; round2-syntax.md's checker-invisible operand errors). Note that the record already contains the pro-regularity half of this asymmetry as a filed hypothesis: round2-syntax's 'precedence misbinding' — writers trained on C-family corpora silently misparse their own infix under a misremembered precedence table, an error class canonical form eliminates by construction. Familiarity's benefit channel and its silent-error channel are the same channel: the prior. Regularity cuts the prior's noise; in-context examples supply its signal.

2. D2'S BUDGET ARITHMETIC FAVORS A POSITIVE SPEC OVER A DIFF SPEC. Because D2 stipulates the language is unknown to models, nothing is inherited for free — every semantic rule must be in-context regardless of surface. A regular grammar (one spelling per construct, tree isomorphic to the checked core, no precedence table, no coercion rules, no exception clauses) minimizes spec-token count as a matter of production-count arithmetic. A familiarity-exploiting spec is necessarily 'Rust minus/plus a diff': it must additionally teach the negative space ('unlike Rust, `+` here...'), and it asks the model to apply negative corrections against a strong prior — the weakest known instruction-following mode (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED here, experiment named below). Regularity also compounds example leverage: with a systematic grammar, N examples cover the construct space compositionally; an exception-laden familiar grammar needs examples per exception (also EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED). On the program budget, the measured cost of canonical form is the 1.35–1.48x proxy-token ratio (round2-syntax, regex proxy on fragments — real-tokenizer whole-program measurement still owed), and the syntax verdict's what-must-be-true #7 already mandates attacking it inside regularity: short fixed operator atoms, flat n-ary nodes. D2 upgrades that clause to first-class, and 'familiarity where free' is its concrete instrument — choose atoms that are single BPE tokens in target tokenizers, which familiar keywords usually are.

3. THE REPAIR LOOP IS THE MECHANISM THAT CLOSES THE GAP, AND ONLY REGULARITY FEEDS IT. The round-2 additions — node-path diagnostics, deterministic local parse recovery, elaboration records, site-enumerated edit lists, content-addressed caching on canonical bytes (D001/D004 compile-time channel) — constitute a compiler-as-teacher loop that converts unfamiliarity into bounded iterations. That entire apparatus is keyed to grammar-tree isomorphism and byte-canonical identity; six verdicts' consistency layer (provenance diff, elaboration diffing, one-definition rules, subtree hashing) sits on the same substrate. The loop can only fix what the checker rejects: prior-driven silent errors produce accepted programs, so no amount of loop engineering recovers them. F001/F003 give the endgame stakes — unchecked writer 'facts' are a miscompilation channel; under D1, silent wrongness is the one unaffordable class.

4. BURDEN OF PROOF. This position requires re-opening no floor: canonical form stays; familiarity enters only as atom choice inside it. Familiarity-exploitation beyond atoms — precedence, sugar, elision, Rust-lookalike operators — requires re-opening the canonical-form floor, which the debate charter permits only with cause, and the only carded cause (token cost) is a fragment-level proxy with its real-tokenizer experiment still pending. F006 (SPARK) is the corpus's precedent that verification-first languages restrict surface expressiveness — and note SPARK is safe precisely because it is a loud SUBSET of Ada; a Rust-lookalike over mode-carrying arithmetic cannot be a subset, only a silent divergence, which is the un-SPARK.

5. WHAT D2 CONCRETELY RESHAPES UNDER THIS POSITION: (a) the spec is a positive closed description, never 'like Rust except'; (b) canonical atoms are selected jointly for single-token BPE cost and loudness-safe familiarity, with every familiar borrowing carrying a documented prior-collision review in the spec; (c) an explicit anti-prior section enumerates the retained silent-zone divergences (modes, drops, capabilities, trap) with contrastive examples; (d) the syntax A/B experiment (already filed) is extended to measure prior-interference, not just token cost.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Verified (3-0): overflow behavior is a per-operation optimizer contract (unflagged add wraps; nuw/nsw make overflow poison). This makes 'same `+` spelling, different overflow semantics than Rust' a miscompile-grade silent error, the central exhibit for forcing alien mode-carrying arithmetic spellings.

Verified: the poison/undef/freeze lattice means wrongly-selected numeric contracts have optimizer-semantic consequences invisible at source; prior-driven mode confusion is not a style bug but a soundness channel.

Verified 3-0: non-interference facts are the core optimizer enablers, so the surface's job is truthful fact transport; unchecked prior-assumed facts are exactly what a familiar-but-divergent surface would smuggle in.

Verified 3-0: violating stated no-alias promises is UB — the stakes of a writer whose prior asserts facts the language did not state; underwrites 'silent wrongness is the unaffordable class' under D1.

Verified 3-0: SPARK is corpus precedent that verification-first languages restrict surface expressiveness, and its safety comes from being a LOUD subset of Ada — the loudness criterion generalizes this; a Rust-lookalike over divergent semantics cannot be a subset.

Verified 2026-07-02 (amended sources): data-race freedom rides ownership/capability checking and manual assertions are the UB channel; Rust's implicit auto-trait prior collides with our explicit checked-capability regime — a named silent-zone item for the anti-prior spec section.

Verified 2026-07-02: monomorphization gives concrete-code performance; with instantiation inference cut, Rust-prior elided instantiations become loud errors — an example of familiarity that is safe to borrow because misuse is checker-visible.

Direct-extraction: compile-time/caching is an admissible AI cost channel; content-addressed caching on canonical bytes is what makes the repair loop cheap enough to be the familiarity-gap-closing mechanism.

Direct-extraction, NOT yet adversarially verified (not in the 2026-07-02 batch): Julia's hidden-type silent performance degradation is the corpus's exemplar of silent divergence being worse than loud rejection; leaned on only for direction, not magnitude.

Direct-extraction, NOT yet verified: conversions perturb dispatch/specialization, supporting the zero-implicit-conversion floor whose surface must not resemble coercion-rich languages (`as`-shaped casts).

The canonical-form floor, the 1.35–1.48x proxy token measurement, the precedence-misbinding hypothesis (familiarity as silent-error source), the gated bijective-codec path, and follow-up #4 (training-penalty decay with in-context examples) — this position's load-bearing experiment was already filed there.

Decided floors (zero implicit conversions, explicit boundary facts) that make most Rust idioms inexpressible-hence-loud; the wrong-but-well-typed error class this position treats as the binding constraint.

The unified consistency layer, machine diagnostics, edit lists, and 'cleanup always runs is NOT an invariant' — the repair-loop apparatus that only functions on canonical form, and the deepest semantic divergence from the Rust prior.

concessions: The position's central premise — that in-context examples plus a deterministic repair loop close the familiarity gap at acceptable iteration cost — is itself EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED; if the syntax A/B shows a large, non-decaying training-distribution penalty on the canonical form, regularity-first pays a persistent per-unit cost and this ruling should be revisited via the already-gated codec path.

Familiar surfaces plausibly lower first-shot parse/syntax defect rates and token-level perplexity, and models may reason better (bug-finding, invariant tracking) over code that looks like training data even when they can parse the alien form — a deeper benefit than syntax that regularity cannot fully replace.

The 1.35–1.48x token cost of canonical form is real and D2 makes it first-class; it also inflates the EXAMPLES section of the spec, so the spec-budget saving from a smaller grammar is partially offset — net spec-budget direction is asserted, not measured.

'Loud' familiarity is not free: every prior-driven banned-construct emission (wildcard arms, closures, elided instantiations) costs a repair iteration, and iteration costs compound with the trap=abort multi-process harness economics the error-handling verdict already flagged.

The loudness criterion requires judging what the prior assigns to a spelling, and the prior is not directly observable; pre-experiment, admission decisions are judgment calls that can err in both directions.

Semantic prior transfer (e.g., relying on Rust drop-for-unlock idioms) is not fully suppressible by surface distance — an alien surface reduces whole-cloth 'this is basically Rust' reasoning but cannot eliminate it; the anti-prior spec section is mitigation, not proof.
risks: Persistent-penalty risk: if the familiarity gap does not decay with in-context examples, every code unit pays elevated repair iterations forever, and D2's program budget is bled by the loop rather than by verbosity.

Reasoning-degradation risk: models may produce semantically worse code over alien-looking programs even at zero parse errors, a cost invisible to the checker and to the currently-filed experiments unless the reasoning-transfer probe is added.

Tokenizer risk: alien operator atoms may fragment into multiple BPE tokens, silently multiplying the measured 1.35–1.48x ratio on real tokenizers; atom selection must be tokenizer-audited or the program budget worsens.

Misjudged-admission risk: a familiar atom admitted as 'loud-safe' may have a silent misuse mode nobody enumerated (e.g., `match` binding-pattern subtleties), reintroducing exactly the prior-driven silent error the position exists to prevent.
Spec-bloat risk: the anti-prior contrastive-example section and per-borrowing collision reviews could grow the spec toward the familiarity spec's size, eroding the claimed one-time budget advantage.

Ecosystem drift risk: toolchain or later debates may bolt familiar renderings onto the canonical core (the second-spelling hazard round2-syntax polices); if the codec ships before its gating A/B, the position's guarantees quietly void.
empiricalUntested: CLAIM: in-context examples + repair loop close the unfamiliar-form defect gap to near-familiar levels within few iterations. TEST: the filed syntax A/B (round2-syntax follow-ups #1/#4) — identical kernels in canonical vs infix/Rust-adjacent form, real model tokenizers, whole programs; measure first-parse success, repair iterations to green, and penalty decay as in-context examples increase.

CLAIM: a familiar surface over divergent semantics induces more SILENT semantic errors than an alien surface over the same semantics. TEST (new, 'prior-collision A/B'): same mode-carrying arithmetic semantics rendered as (a) Rust-lookalike `+` with mode declared out-of-band vs (b) alien per-node `add.wrap`-style ops; differential-test against reference implementations; measure silent wrong-mode rate and checker-caught rate.

CLAIM: negative-space specification ('unlike Rust, ...') is complied with at lower rates than positive closed specification. TEST (new, 'diff-spec adherence'): teach the same semantics via positive regular spec vs Rust-diff spec at matched token budgets; measure violation rates specifically at the diffed rules.

CLAIM: regular grammars yield better few-shot compositional generalization per example than exception-laden familiar grammars. TEST (new): vary example count for both grammar styles; measure defect rate on held-out construct combinations never shown in examples.

CLAIM: models reason worse (bug-finding, invariant tracking, edit correctness) over alien-looking code even when parsing is solved. TEST (new, 'reasoning-transfer probe'): identical programs in both renderings; measure injected-bug detection and read-modify-write correctness with parse errors excluded.

CLAIM: surface similarity modulates semantic prior transfer (a Rust-shaped surface increases reliance on Rust drop/cleanup/auto-trait semantics the language does not have). TEST (new): probe tasks requiring correct behavior under 'cleanup not guaranteed' and explicit-capability rules, run over familiar-shaped vs alien-shaped renderings; measure prior-consistent-but-wrong outputs.

CLAIM: familiar keyword atoms are single tokens in target BPE vocabularies while candidate alien operator atoms are not, and this materially moves the program budget. TEST: tokenizer audit across target models as part of the mandated real-tokenizer measurement (already owed by round2-syntax).

CAVEAT carried per caveats.md: D009 and D010, cited directionally above, are direct-extraction cards not in the 2026-07-02 adversarial verification batch; conclusions leaning on them must be re-checked when they are verified.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: SPARK is the corpus's strongest carded precedent that RESTRICTING the language surface is a viable route to hard guarantees (side-effect-free expressions, ownership-policy access types, anti-aliasing). A hard spec budget is the same architectural move applied to D2: restriction as the mechanism, teachability instead of provability as the guaranteed property. NOTE: this is an analogy at the pattern level — F006's scope is verification, not in-context learning; the transfer is flagged EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED below.

The canonical-form verdict already decided one-spelling-per-construct, grammar isomorphic to the checked tree, no sugar, no precedence tables. Every one of those cuts is simultaneously a spec-token cut: a precedence table, each sugar form, and each alternate spelling would each be a spec section plus examples. The verdict also demonstrates token costs are MEASURABLE (the 1.35-1.48x proxy at scratchpad tokcount.py), which is the precondition for making spec cost a gate criterion rather than a vibe. The budget retroactively strengthens this floor and permanently closes its open infix-codec question: a second write-path surface is a second grammar section in the writable-subset spec, so it now breaks the budget by construction.

The zero-staging verdict is the existence proof that this project already accepts the budget's architectural pattern: a closed, decidable, trivially-terminating constant sublanguage IN the spec, with unbounded expressive power (external generators + provenance diffing, schedule search) relocated to the TOOLCHAIN. A Turing-complete comptime would have been the single largest spec-budget item imaginable — a second evaluation semantics to teach in-context. The budget generalizes this verdict's logic into a uniform gate: anything whose teaching cost exceeds its writable-surface necessity moves toolchain-side.

Three ways. (1) The core sketch plus 'Additions required because the writer is an AI' already accumulates an unbudgeted teaching load: ownership/aliasing capabilities, effect rows, numeric modes, layout annotations, contract groups, capability predicates, lifetime contracts, provenance records, Result taxonomy — each a spec line item nobody priced. The synthesis's own 'consistency-layer proliferation vs coherence' and 'periphery accreting into a second, evidence-free language' tensions are exactly what an aggregate cap detects and forbids. (2) Its compiler-generated token-compact interface views, machine-readable node-path diagnostics, and site-enumerated edit lists are the compiler-as-teacher channels that make a small writable-subset spec feasible. (3) Its null-hypothesis-CUT procedure is precedent that a hard default (cut unless justified) produces a coherent core where per-feature soft weighing would not.

Monomorphization keeps generics' spec cost low relative to their program-token savings: one template mechanism to teach, no bound-solving/inference semantics in the writable subset (explicit instantiation was already decided). Generics is the worked example of the joint spec+program accounting the gate must use: its spec delta is small and its per-program savings are O(N copies), so it passes the gate comfortably — showing the budget does not mechanically kill high-value features.

Compile time and toolchain machinery are carded as ADMISSIBLE cost channels (with D0). The budget's central architectural move — displacing complexity from in-context spec tokens (scarce, paid in every window) to toolchain mechanism (admissible, paid in silicon) — pushes cost in the direction the corpus already sanctions.

InstCombine/Alive2 discipline prices every semantic rule at proof-grade validation cost. Every rule in the writable-subset spec is also trusted verification surface, so the spec-token budget and the verification-surface budget point the same direction: a spec that fits 10-20k tokens is also a semantics small enough to validate rule-by-rule. The budget is not in tension with the verification charter; it is the same minimization under a second metric.

Halide's schedule layer is carded evidence that optimization power can be exposed as checkable declarative STRUCTURE rather than staged language machinery, with its negative scope denying transfer of the staged-DSL machinery to a general language. Precedent that 'expressive power without spec growth' is achievable: the arrays-loops schedule layer, if adopted, adds a small declarative vocabulary to the spec while search/autotuning lives in tooling — the budget-compatible shape.

Send/Sync-class capability predicates achieve D1-class data-race prevention through a tiny closed writer-facing vocabulary (two predicates plus borrowing) — carded evidence that safety machinery of the required strength CAN have a compact teachable surface, which is what makes 'D1 machinery fits inside a hard budget' plausible rather than fantasy. CAVEAT carried per the synthesis: C004 is a provisional direct extraction, not workflow-confirmed, and is already flagged for adversarial verification.

Swift SIL's lesson — performance-critical semantics should be visible to TOOLS rather than buried in implicit source behavior — supports the budget's writable-subset/full-spec split: the full normative semantics (drop elaboration, lowering contracts, fact provenance) lives below the writer surface in toolchain artifacts of unbounded size, while the writer-facing spec stays small. The elaborated-artifact re-read channel the synthesis already mandates is the delivery mechanism.

concessions: The exact 10-20k figure is uncalibrated. Nothing in the corpus establishes that the round-2 core (ownership + effects + modes + contract kernel + canonical examples) compresses into 10-20k tokens; if an honest draft measures at 35k, a pre-committed 15k cap would force either destructive cuts into D1-mandatory machinery or dishonest compression. The defensible commitments are (a) hardness of the gate and (b) small-language order of magnitude; the number itself must be set by a draft-spec measurement pass and then frozen, not asserted a priori. My 'Lua/Scheme-class' anchor is itself an uncarded token estimate.

The gate is subordinate to the directives, not above them: D1 machinery cannot be cut by budget fiat. The honest form of the position is lexicographic — D1-mandatory spec content is inside the budget by construction, and the gate ranks everything ELSE for the remaining tokens. 'Regardless of round-1/2 merits' applies to feature merits, never to directive compliance.

A pure spec-token gate optimizes the wrong margin in isolation. Spec cost is one-time per context window; program cost recurs per code unit. A feature with a 1k-token spec delta that saves 10k program tokens per unit (generics is exactly this) must pass; one that saves nothing must not. The gate is only sound as a JOINT spec-delta + amortized-program-delta + displacement analysis, which concedes ground to the 'budgets must be traded, not capped' opposition — I keep the hard aggregate cap but concede admission ranking inside it must be joint-cost-based.

Spec tokens are not spec clarity. A hard cap creates Goodhart pressure toward terse, ambiguous normative prose that models misread; equal-token specs can differ wildly in teaching effectiveness (rule-heavy vs example-heavy). The gate therefore cannot be budget-only: it must be budget PLUS a measured writer-accuracy floor on a fixed task battery, or the cap actively damages D2's goal.

If compiler-as-teacher repair loops prove cheap and effective, the marginal value of a HARD static-spec cap drops — the effective spec becomes spec + diagnostic corpus, and progressive disclosure could tolerate a larger reference spec with a small working subset. I hold that the cap is still the conservative choice (repair loops burn wall-clock and per-unit tokens, and their effectiveness is exactly as untested as my claims), but this is a genuine fork, not a refuted alternative.

The budget leaks unless it covers the writer-visible TOTAL contract. Relabeling a feature 'toolchain' does not free its tokens if the writer must still be taught the tool's interaction protocol (provenance record format, edit-list format, elaborated-artifact reading). The cap must be defined over everything the writer must hold in context to operate, or 'move it to the toolchain' becomes an accounting trick — this materially constrains how much relief toolchain relocation actually buys.

The cap is calibrated to current-generation in-context learning. Long-context degradation is model-generation-dependent; the constraint may relax substantially within the language's lifetime. Mitigation, not rebuttal: budgets relax monotonically at low cost (freed tokens are pure surplus), while retro-tightening an overgrown spec is a breaking redesign — so binding early errs on the recoverable side.
result.coreArgument:
D2 converts spec size from an aesthetic into a physics: the spec is re-paid in every context window that writes code, competing byte-for-byte with the task's own program context, repair diagnostics, and re-read artifacts. Without a HARD cap, spec cost is an externality — and round 2 is the demonstration. Each verdict is locally impeccable, yet the synthesis accretes an unpriced teaching load: ownership/aliasing capabilities, effect rows, numeric modes, layout annotations, contract groups, capability predicates, lifetime contracts, Result-vs-trap taxonomy, provenance formats — plus its own admitted tensions ('consistency-layer proliferation vs coherence', a periphery 'accreting into a second, evidence-free language'). Every feature is individually justified; the aggregate is unbudgeted because no debate owns the sum. That is a commons problem, and commons problems are solved by caps, not sentiments. The project already proved this procedurally: round 2's null-hypothesis-CUT default produced a coherent minimal core precisely because the burden sat on features, not on the cutter. The budget gate is the same discipline with a measurable currency: every feature ships a measured spec delta (grammar + typing rules + annotation semantics + minimal canonical examples for correct EMISSION) into a maintained spec-cost ledger, and admission requires fitting the cap or displacing something — an amendment to decision-gates.md adding spec-cost to the design_candidate promotion requirements alongside evidence, contract, threat model, and validation.

Why hard rather than soft: (1) soft targets do not bind under adversarial feature pressure — every future proposal will be individually justified, exactly as every round-2 feature was, so only a global cap forces cross-feature tradeoffs to be argued at all; (2) the asymmetry favors binding early — relaxing a cap later is free (surplus tokens), while tightening an overgrown spec is a breaking redesign of a shipped language; (3) a hard cap is the only form that changes DESIGN behavior rather than documentation behavior — a soft target gets met by moving text to appendices, a hard cap on the writable subset gets met by making the language smaller or the toolchain smarter.

What the gate forces architecturally — six concrete consequences: (1) THE WRITABLE-SUBSET SPLIT becomes a first-class artifact: the budgeted spec is what a writer needs to emit correct code; the full normative semantics (lowering contracts, drop elaboration, fact provenance, verifier rules) lives below the surface in unbounded toolchain artifacts, per F009's facts-visible-to-tools lesson. Rules that are checked-not-chosen are taught by machine-readable diagnostics at violation time — the compiler-as-teacher channels round 2 already mandates (node-path diagnostics, site-enumerated edit lists, token-compact interface views) get budget relief, aligning incentives with infrastructure already ordered. (2) REGULARITY AS COMPRESSION: a D1-strength language fits a small spec only if rules are schema-like — one binding form, one call form, one annotation frame shared by ownership, effects, and modes — so the budget hardens canonical form (round2-syntax) from a drift-detection decision into a compression requirement, and permanently closes the infix-codec reopener: a second surface is a second grammar section, unaffordable by construction. (3) CLOSED SMALL VOCABULARIES: the annotation vocabulary is the biggest line item, so numeric modes stay few and named (N002-style), capability predicates stay C004-small, and the contract-group kernel stays minimal — open-ended vocabularies are per-item spec costs and the ledger makes each addition visible. (4) TOOLCHAIN RELOCATION AS DEFAULT: the metaprogramming verdict is the template — closed constant sublanguage in-spec, unbounded generation in provenance-checked external tooling (A006's structure-not-staging pattern); the budget generalizes: any capability teachable as a tool invocation rather than a semantics moves out, in the D0/D004-admissible cost direction. (5) PERIPHERY POLICING: the FFI wall's writable surface is inside the budget, so the synthesis's 'second, evidence-free language' failure mode becomes a measurable violation rather than an unowned drift. (6) VERIFICATION ALIGNMENT: per N006, every spec rule is proof-priced trusted surface — the token-minimal spec and the minimal validated semantics are the same object minimized under two metrics; the budget costs the verification charter nothing and buys it a second enforcement channel.

Precedence and mechanics, stated honestly: the gate ranks below the directives (D1 machinery is inside-budget by fiat; the cap disciplines everything competing for the remainder), admission inside the cap is ranked by joint spec-delta plus amortized program-delta (which is why generics, D001, passes trivially and a comptime evaluator never could), and the numeric cap is set by measuring an honest draft spec of the round-2 core — then frozen with pre-committed review conditions. F006 is the carded pattern precedent: SPARK bought true-by-construction guarantees by restricting the surface; this language buys in-context teachability the same way. The alternative — 'weigh spec cost case by case' — is the null hypothesis this project already rejected once, for the same reason: unweighted commons always overgraze.
empiricalUntested: FOUNDATIONAL: that model code-generation accuracy degrades as in-context spec size grows, holding normative content fixed. The corpus has zero AI-codegen cards (round-2 synthesis states this explicitly), so the budget's core premise is uncarded. Experiment (SPEC-SIZE DOSE-RESPONSE): render the same kernel-language spec at ~5k/10k/20k/40k tokens (varying redundancy, examples, prose density), hold tasks fixed, measure first-compile success, escaped-semantic-error rate, and repair iterations across models; the curve's knee calibrates the cap and its flatness would falsify hardness.

That 10-20k tokens suffices to teach D1-class ownership/borrowing plus effects/modes in-context to a fixed accuracy floor. Experiment (D1 TEACHABILITY FLOOR): write the minimal ownership+capabilities spec section with canonical examples; measure borrow/capability-violation rates on generation tasks vs spec size; control for Rust-prior transfer by testing both Rust-adjacent and alien surface spellings, since transfer inflates apparent teachability of exactly this machinery.

That compiler-as-teacher diagnostics can substitute for spec sections at acceptable loop cost. Experiment (SPEC ABLATION): remove checked-not-chosen rule sections from the in-context spec, rely on machine-readable node-path diagnostics; measure iterations-to-green, wall-clock, per-unit token spend, and escaped-error rate vs full-spec baseline. This decides how much budget relief the writable-subset split actually delivers and directly informs D2's progressive-disclosure open question.

That token COUNT is the right budgeted quantity rather than spec composition. Experiment (COMPOSITION AT FIXED BUDGET): equal-token specs at rule-heavy vs example-heavy vs mixed ratios; measure accuracy. If composition dominates count, the gate must budget a (count, composition, accuracy-floor) triple, not a scalar.

That a hard cap outperforms joint soft optimization of spec+program budgets at system level. Experiment (GATE POLICY SIMULATION): take the round-2 core plus a queue of candidate features with measured spec/program deltas; compare capped-lexicographic vs weighted-sum admission on total tokens-per-correct-code-unit across a task battery. This is the honest test of my position against the strongest rival.

That familiarity leverage changes the required budget (D2's delegated question): same semantics under training-distribution-adjacent vs alien canonical surface; measure spec tokens required to reach the fixed accuracy floor. The budget position is agnostic on the answer but its calibration is hostage to it.

Inherited flags this position leans on, still open from round 2: the drift-vs-verbosity hypothesis and training-distribution penalty decay (round2-syntax follow-ups), repair-loop asymmetry on literal-source diagnostics (round2-metaprogramming follow-up), and C004's provisional status pending adversarial verification — if C004 falls, my 'compact capability vocabulary' evidence weakens and the D1-teachability line item must be re-estimated upward.
result.position: Hard spec-size budget as a decision gate: the complete writable-subset spec (grammar + typing rules + annotation vocabulary + canonical examples) must fit a fixed token budget of small-language order (~10-20k tokens, Lua/Scheme-class), and any feature whose measured spec delta breaks the budget is cut or relocated to the toolchain regardless of its round-1/2 feature merits — spec cost becomes a promotion criterion in decision-gates.md alongside optimizer necessity and verification necessity, subordinate only to D0/D1/D2 themselves.
risks: Goodhart compression: the cap is met by terse ambiguous prose instead of a smaller language, degrading writer accuracy while the metric reads green. Mitigation must be built into the ruling: the gate is (token cap AND measured accuracy floor on a frozen task battery); a spec edit that passes the cap but drops the floor fails the gate.

Accounting leakage: features relabeled 'toolchain' whose interaction protocols still consume writer context (provenance record formats, generator invocation, elaborated-artifact reading conventions), silently un-capping the budget. The cap must be defined over the writer-visible total contract, and the ledger must carry toolchain-protocol line items with the same rigor as grammar line items.

Miscalibration against D1: if the honest minimal spelling of ownership+capabilities+effects+modes alone approaches or exceeds the cap, the gate either forces fictional compression of safety-critical rules (worst outcome: models systematically misapply borrow rules taught too tersely, producing exactly the coherently-hallucinated-but-well-typed code D1 machinery exists to stop) or gets quietly ignored, discrediting gating generally. Mitigation: set the number AFTER the draft-spec measurement pass; pre-commit that D1 content is never the compression target.

Wrong-margin cuts: a scalar spec gate applied naively could reject small-spec-delta features with large per-program savings or admit cheap-spec features that bloat every program (interacting badly with the open interior-annotation regime — if mandatory interior annotations win their experiment, program budgets swell and the optimal spec/program split shifts). The gate must run the joint accounting or it optimizes the one-time budget at the expense of the recurring one.

Ossification: a frozen cap plus displacement rule raises the political cost of later well-justified additions (e.g., if the interfaces experiment resolves toward bounded quantification needing richer surface, or the F009 fourth-model axis needs writer-visible sharing annotations). Mitigation: versioned budget reviews with pre-committed reopening conditions, mirroring the corpus's existing reopener discipline.

Model-generation obsolescence: the cap encodes 2026 in-context-learning limits into permanent language architecture; if long-context reliability improves sharply, the language may be smaller than it needed to be. Partially self-correcting (relaxation is cheap and monotonic; freed budget is surplus), but features already cut and designed-around are not free to re-add.

Process risk: the spec-cost ledger adds measurement overhead to every future debate, and token counts are tokenizer-dependent — the ledger must pin tokenizer versions (same discipline caveats.md demands for version-sensitive sources) or the budget becomes unreproducible and gameable.

result.position: No hard spec-size target. Keep the round-2 core as ruled. Charter, immediately, a maximally compressed, example-paired, machine-oriented, toolchain-verified spec format plus a spec-token provenance ledger with pre-committed thresholds — and let measured in-context learning curves (a new T-series evidence lane) decide if, where, and at what number any budget ever becomes law. A cap adopted today is a design decision made from zero cards; a cap adopted from measured curves is the only kind this project's evidence discipline permits.
result.coreArgument:
ARGUMENT 1 — A hard cap today is procedurally forbidden by the project's own standing rule. caveats.md requires that every design proposal cite exact evidence cards and state what remains unproven, and warns against premature language-design decisions. A numeric spec budget is a major design decision whose every load-bearing premise is an uncarded model-behavior claim: that a correctness-vs-spec-size threshold exists, where it sits, that token COUNT rather than spec organization/example quality is the binding variable, and that correctness degrades with spec size at all in the relevant range. The corpus contains zero AI-codegen evidence cards — every round-2 verdict states this verbatim. Round 2 already established the governance template for exactly this situation: the infix write-codec was neither adopted nor banned but parked behind a gated A/B experiment with pre-registered metrics (round2-syntax.md follow-ups; the round2_empirical backlog entry that already orders measurement of 'training-distribution penalty decay with in-context examples'). The metaprogramming verdict did the same with its workload census and pre-committed reopening conditions. The spec-size target deserves identical treatment: park the number behind the learning-curve experiment. Note that D2 itself (user-directives.md) lists 'hard spec-size target' as an OPEN question delegated to this round — D2 makes spec tokens a first-class constraint; it does not entail a cap. First-class constraint means measured, ledgered, and optimized — not fixed by negotiation before the first measurement exists.

ARGUMENT 2 — The option-value asymmetry is decisive. Declining a cap forecloses nothing: if measured curves reveal a cliff, a budget can be adopted then, derived from data, and the same experiments identify WHAT to cut — which spec sections consume tokens without buying correctness. Adopting a cap now forces architectural surgery immediately (that is precisely what 'what does it force architecturally' means), and every cut it forces is made against zero evidence. Wrong-number risk is asymmetric: a too-generous cap is inert theater that does no design work; a too-tight cap binds against machinery that is not discretionary (Argument 3). A reversible non-decision dominates an irreversible guess.

ARGUMENT 3 — If a cap binds, it can only bind against D1 and the decided floors. Round 2 already ran feature essentialism under a CUT null hypothesis: everything not optimizer- or verification-necessary is gone — closures, macros, dyn dispatch, unwinding, module semantics, sugar, implicit conversions, cross-boundary inference (round2-synthesis-minimal-core.md cut list). What remains is the safety and verification spine D1 makes non-negotiable: ownership/aliasing capabilities (F001), Send/Sync-class capability predicates (C004), effect rows, named numeric modes (N001/N002), the contract kernel, the consistency-checking layer. A binding spec cap has no discretionary fat left to consume; its pressure lands directly on decided floors — canonical form, checked ownership, zero implicit conversions — which this debate is forbidden to reopen without cause. The opposition's claim that 'a budget forces architectural discipline' is backwards: the discipline already happened in round 2, under a sharper criterion (soundness necessity, card-cited) than token count. A token cap is a cruder instrument applied after a sharper one has already cut to the bone; the only new cuts it can force are the ones the sharper instrument refused.

ARGUMENT 4 — Spec size is the wrong variable; the round-2 core is already spec-minimal BY CONSTRUCTION, and the remaining leverage is spec engineering, which needs no cap to authorize. What makes real language specs enormous is exactly the material round 2 cut: overload resolution and precedence tables (gone with infix), coercion lattices (zero implicit conversions), macro hygiene and expansion order (zero staging), trait-solver and bound-inference behavior (explicit instantiation, no solving in the trust path), unwinding semantics (trap=abort), and inference algorithms (zero cross-boundary inference — there is no algorithm to teach; the fully-elaborated artifact shows every fact explicitly, F009's visibility lesson applied to teaching). One spelling per construct means one rule per construct documented once, and a grammar isomorphic to the checked tree means the syntax chapter and the semantics chapter are the same chapter. F006 (SPARK) and N006 (Alive2-grade pricing of every rewrite rule) already align spec economy with the design: fewer semantics = fewer rules = fewer proof obligations = fewer spec tokens, achieved by construction rather than by cap. The untouched leverage is FORMAT, all of it toolchain work requiring no language change: (a) typing rules as deduplicated machine-oriented schemas; (b) every rule paired with a minimal positive/negative program pair that is itself a checked, content-addressed artifact — spec examples compiled and verified against the toolchain on every build, reusing mechanism 2 of the consistency layer, so the spec can never drift from the implementation (a provenance-checked spec); (c) compiler-generated task-scoped spec slices via the same machinery that already generates token-compact interface views on demand (modules replacement, synthesis line 59) — progressive disclosure as generated toolchain output, not as language redesign; (d) the already-mandated compiler-as-teacher loop (machine-readable node-path diagnostics, site-enumerated edit lists, elaboration records) which moves teaching load out of the one-time spec and into the repair loop, where it is paid only on error. Optimize the spec; the language was already optimized.

ARGUMENT 5 — Any hard number has an unstable denominator. A cap must be denominated in a context size and a cost model, both exogenous and moving: deployment context windows, prompt-caching amortization of one-time spec cost, retrieval quality over long contexts. Fixing the number today calibrates the language's permanent architecture to a transient infrastructure snapshot, on the eve of the very experiments that would price it. The correct instrument for a constraint whose binding point is unknown and moving is a measured ledger with alarms and pre-committed trigger thresholds — the exact pattern this project already uses for compile-loop latency (D001/D004 channel: measured, cached, thresholded — never capped a priori) and for every other unpriced cost in the synthesis's empirical program.

WHAT THIS POSITION BINDS ITSELF TO (so it is neither soft nor unfalsifiable): (1) SPEC-TOKEN LEDGER — every spec section carries provenance to the verdict or rule that requires it; CI tracks total and per-section counts on real model tokenizers (not the regex proxy the syntax round already flagged as inadequate); growth requires named justification; the ledger counts ALL in-context teaching artifacts (spec, examples, diagnostic vocabularies), not just the spec file, closing the Goodhart hole where a capped 'spec' silently exports its bulk into uncapped examples. (2) T-SERIES EVIDENCE LANE, gating spec hardening at the same blocking status as the flagship drift experiment: the teach-from-spec ladder and its siblings (named under empiricalUntested below), with pre-committed thresholds — if correctness at the ledger's actual size is materially below correctness at a compressed size, a budget becomes law at the measured knee, and that ruling names which floor, if any, must reopen with cause. (3) SPEC-FORMAT CHARTER NOW — compressed machine-oriented format, checked example pairs, generated slices — because none of it waits on the experiments and all of it improves every experimental arm.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: D2's own text delegates 'hard spec-size target' to this round as an OPEN question alongside progressive disclosure and familiarity leverage; D2 mandates spec/program token budgets as first-class CONSTRAINTS, which is satisfied by measurement+ledger+thresholds and does not entail a fixed cap.

Requires exact card citation and explicit unproven-claims statements for every design proposal, and exists to prevent premature design decisions; a numeric cap adopted before any teachability card exists violates it directly.

Governance template: the infix codec was parked behind a gated A/B experiment with pre-registered metrics rather than decided; the training-distribution penalty was admitted as an admissible-but-unquantified cost; the 1.35-1.48x token measurement was run BEFORE any mandate — measure-then-decide is the established pattern for token-economics questions.

Second instance of the template: workload census plus pre-committed reopening conditions instead of an a-priori capacity ruling; also carries F006's restriction precedent showing surface restriction (not budget caps) is the carded route to small verifiable semantics.

The CUT null hypothesis already removed everything not optimizer/verification-necessary, so a binding cap could only press on D1-mandatory machinery or decided floors; line 59's compiler-generated token-compact interface views are existing progressive-disclosure machinery reusable for task-scoped spec slices; the entire 'Empirical research needed' section is the measure-then-harden pipeline this position extends to spec size.

Shows the project already treats token economics as measure-then-decide: the O(k^2) annotation-cost claim and elaborated-views mitigation were routed to experiments with the verdict left needs_evidence — the same discipline applied here yields a T-series lane, not a cap.

SPARK achieves verification goals by RESTRICTING semantics to a small fragment; restriction simultaneously shrinks the documentable surface — spec economy arrives by construction from the round-2 design, independent of any token cap.

Alive2-grade validation prices every added rule/rewrite at proof-grade cost, already forcing rule-count minimality; rule count, not a token number, is the principled driver of spec size, and it is already governed.

Swift SIL's lesson — keep performance-critical semantics visible rather than implicit — applied to teachability: the fully-elaborated canonical artifact means the spec never has to teach hidden inference or coercion algorithms, the single largest bulk item in human language specs.

With C004, identifies the non-discretionary D1 spec content (ownership/aliasing capabilities, Send/Sync-class predicates) that a binding cap would pressure — demonstrating a cap's only bite is against constraints this debate cannot trade away.

Capability-predicate kernel is required core (and known provisional); its spec weight is D1-mandatory, so it cannot be sacrificed to a token cap — and its provisional status shows the corpus itself is still too unsettled to fix numeric envelopes.

With D004: compile-time/iteration-loop cost is the precedent 'admissible AI cost channel' — handled by measurement, content-addressed caching, and pre-committed thresholds, never by an a-priori hard cap; spec tokens are the same class of cost and merit the same instrument.

Documents that optimization/compile-time tradeoffs are managed as measured tradeoffs with tooling (codegen units, LTO), not fixed budgets — the architectural pattern this position transplants to spec economics.

The experiment measuring 'training-distribution penalty decay with in-context examples' is ALREADY ordered and blocks hardening round-2 verdicts; the spec-size learning-curve ladder is a natural extension of an existing gate, so no new governance machinery is needed to adopt my position.

Halide precedent: optimization choices as checkable structure with search in the tooling loop, negative scope denying transfer of staged machinery — supports pushing spec-economics work (slicing, example generation, format search) into the toolchain rather than reshaping the language.

concessions: D2 is law and the spec-token constraint is real: this position must never be read as 'spec size is free' or as a re-litigation of D2. I accept a mandatory tokenizer-real ledger, CI regression tracking, and pre-committed thresholds that can institute a hard budget later. The disagreement with the opposition is fixed-in-advance versus derived-from-curves, not whether spec economics govern design.

The opposition's strongest point stands: without a forcing number, specs historically accrete, and this project is demonstrably not immune — the synthesis's own open tension shows the FFI periphery 'accreting into a second, evidence-free language' with no procedural block. A provenance ledger is my answer, and I concede it is weaker than a cap if governance discipline lapses.

If a real capability cliff exists BELOW the round-2 core's irreducible spec size, the measure-first route discovers it later than a cap-first design would, and late discovery is more expensive: spec hardening and toolchain work will have begun, and re-opening floors after hardening costs more than designing under a cap from day one.

The one-time-cost amortization argument (prompt caching makes spec cost nearly free per code unit) is an infrastructure assumption about deployment workflows, not a carded fact; in deployment contexts without caching, spec cost recurs per session and the opposition's arithmetic strengthens.
A hard cap has one honest virtue a ledger cannot fully replicate: it is a self-executing commitment device visible to every future debate without ongoing enforcement will.

My compressed-spec program rests on untested claims of its own (that machine-oriented, example-paired formats teach better per token) — symmetric with the opposition's untested cliff claims; both sides' claims route through the same T-series experiments, which is itself an argument for running them before ruling.

The program-token half of D2 is untouched by spec-side compression: per-code-unit verbosity (elaborated artifacts, explicit instantiations, annotation weight) has its own cost structure and its own already-ordered experiments (O(k^2) token economics, elaborated-view re-read rates); nothing here answers it, and my position must not be used to dodge it.
risks: Ratchet erosion: under delivery pressure the ledger is ignored, spec grows monotonically, and by the time curves are measured the political cost of cutting is prohibitive — the no-cap regime degenerates into no-constraint in practice.

Measurement deferral becomes permanent: 'wait for the curves' can be a stalling tactic; if the T-series lane is not resourced and not made a blocking gate (same status as the flagship drift experiment), the language ships with an unteachable spec and D2 is violated retroactively.

Cross-model non-transfer: learning curves measured on current model families may mis-size what deployment-era models need; a data-derived budget can still be wrong. (This risk afflicts a hard cap at least as severely — a negotiated number is calibrated to nothing — but it caps how much confidence measurement can ever deliver.)

Wrong-artifact-boundary risk: optimizing 'the spec' invites silently moving teaching load into examples, diagnostics vocabulary, or tool documentation that still consume context; if the ledger fails to count all in-context teaching artifacts, my position produces the illusion of spec economy while total teaching cost grows.

Discontinuous growth at open decision points: if the generics/interfaces tension resolves toward richer machinery (bounded quantification, full contract groups), spec size jumps; a no-cap regime has no automatic brake at that moment, so the T-series thresholds must be explicitly re-run at every such resolution or the ledger silently absorbs the jump.

If the learning-curve experiments themselves are noisy or model-version-unstable, the pre-committed thresholds may never fire cleanly, leaving the project in permanent 'measuring' state — the failure mode where evidence discipline becomes evidence paralysis.
empiricalUntested: E1 (curve shape): model program-correctness as a function of spec token count is smooth or flat in the relevant range — no cliff. UNTESTED; no card. Experiment T1 'teach-from-spec ladder': one fixed task battery, spec variants at ~{0.25x, 0.5x, 1x, 2x} of the drafted size, across >=3 model families; measure first-parse success, semantic-error rate vs a reference implementation, repair iterations to green, total tokens.

E2 (example load-bearing): example-paired rules teach more correctness per token than prose rules, so cap-driven cutting of examples would be maximally destructive. UNTESTED. Experiment T2: token-matched spec variants — rules-only vs examples-only vs paired — on the T1 battery.

E3 (progressive disclosure): task-scoped generated spec slices plus the compiler-as-teacher repair loop match full-spec-in-context correctness at lower total token cost. UNTESTED (D2 itself lists this open). Experiment T3: full-spec vs sliced+repair-loop arms, measuring iterations-to-green and total spend including repair-loop traffic.

E4 (familiarity leverage — cuts AGAINST my position): models need materially more spec/example tokens to reach parity on the alien canonical form than on a training-distribution-adjacent surface. UNTESTED; rides the already-ordered syntax A/B experiment ('training-distribution penalty decay with in-context examples', missing-research-backlog round2_empirical).

E5 (attention degradation): long specs degrade retrieval of individual rules during generation for this artifact class ('lost-in-the-middle' behavior on spec-shaped contexts). Plausible from general literature but UNCARDED in this corpus. Experiment T4: rule-retrieval probes embedded in generation tasks at varying spec lengths and rule positions.

E6 (amortization economics): one-time spec cost is effectively amortized by prompt caching in realistic agentic repair-loop workflows, making per-code-unit spec cost near zero. UNTESTED as workflow economics. Experiment T5: instrument a real multi-turn repair workflow; measure marginal spec-token cost per code unit with and without caching.

E7 (flagged for symmetry — the opposition's implicit claim): that any specific proposed cap number corresponds to a real model-capability threshold. UNTESTED and currently untestable except via T1 — which is exactly the argument for running T1 before legislating the number.

E8 (my spec-format claim): machine-oriented deduplicated rule schemas with checked positive/negative example pairs are learned faster per token by models than conventional prose specs. UNTESTED. Experiment T6: format A/B within fixed token budgets on the T1 battery — this gates my own 'optimize the spec' charter and I accept it as a gate.

result.position: Full annotation everywhere, compressed: the writer carries the annotation weight in source at proof-carrying density, and the token bill is paid down by designing the annotation vocabulary and grammar for token economy — short canonical fact atoms (modes fused into operator atoms), factored declarations (named effect rows, named instantiations, contract-group references), and at most one declared default per fact dimension per unit head with zero implicit variation. Semantic facts are never elided from the stored artifact; compression is always syntactic factoring, never inference. Per-program token policy: pay a token wherever a fact binds; save tokens only in how the fact is spelled.
result.coreArgument:
THE ONE-ARTIFACT ARGUMENT (dissolves a standing open tension). Round-2 already decided the stored artifact is fully elaborated — every type/mode/effect/contract explicit, one spelling, byte-canonical (round2-syntax, round2-synthesis SURFACE bullet). The only open question was whether the WRITER emits that density or a sparse form the compiler elaborates. The synthesis itself flags what the sparse route costs (open tension, round2-synthesis-minimal-core.md ~line 113): if the writer emits non-elaborated text while the language stores elaborated form, the elaborator IS a write-path codec and inherits the full policing regime the syntax verdict imposed — bijectivity, version pinning, parse-print-parse fixpoint, canonical-only echo — and neither verdict can say which artifact is the source of record during a repair loop. Full annotation in source makes writer-emitted text, stored artifact, checked tree, and re-read artifact the SAME object: no codec, no bijectivity proof program, no dual source-of-record, diagnostics point into the text the model actually wrote. Under D2 this is also a SPEC-budget win: you teach one form of the language, not a write dialect plus a read dialect plus the mapping between them.

THE D2 READ-SIDE ARGUMENT (why elision's savings are smaller than they look). Round-2's own rulings make a compiler-elaborated fully-annotated view the MANDATORY re-read artifact under any elision regime — any workflow reading raw source without the elaborator is out of contract (round2-type-inference AI-writer section; round2-synthesis 'Additions' bullet 2). So in a read-modify-write loop the model pays elaborated-density tokens on every re-read regardless of regime; elision saves only the write-side delta, while adding the codec, the second dialect, and the source-of-record ambiguity. Whether that write-side delta matters is exactly the unmeasured read:write token ratio (flagged below). Full-annotation-compressed attacks the SAME token bill at the only place both regimes must pay — the spelling — via mechanisms the corpus has already blessed: the syntax verdict's canonical grammar is explicitly required to 'minimize gratuitous verbosity without adding alternate spellings (short fixed operator atoms...)' (round2-syntax, What-must-be-true); the modules verdict already grants compiler-GENERATED token-compact interface views with zero semantic weight for read-context compression (round2-modules). My position is the systematic execution of those two bullets across the whole fact vocabulary, with the division of labor made principled: the COMPILER compresses derived read views (omitting bodies, never facts); the WRITER's source keeps every fact, spelled short.

THE UNIFORMITY ARGUMENT (D2 favors the smallest RULE, not the smallest text). Boundary facts are already mandatorily explicit by decided floor (round2-type-inference floors: every signature/interface/instantiation carries ownership, effect row, numeric mode, layout), so the annotation vocabulary must be taught in-context under ANY regime — the marginal spec cost of 'interiors too' is one sentence. The elision alternative instead requires the spec to convey a deterministic, defaulting-free, ambiguity-rejecting local synthesis well enough that the model can predict what its own elided code means and when annotation is required vs forbidden — a decision procedure, paid once in spec tokens and repeatedly in repair iterations when the model gets it wrong. 'Every binding states its fact' is a uniform rule with no decision procedure. (Whether models follow uniform-density rules more reliably than conditional-elision rules is EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED — experiment named below.)

THE REDUNDANCY ARGUMENT (D2 raises the value of checked redundancy).
